does: This paper introduces ProSocialAlign, a test-time framework that improves language model safety by steering generation toward responses that are simultaneously safe, empathetic, and value-aligned without modifying model weights. The method formalizes safety as lexicographic constrained generation: first applying hard constraints to eliminate harmful outputs, then optimizing for prosocial quality within the safe set. It combines directional regulation—subtracting a learned harm vector in parameter space to mitigate harmful continuations—with preference-aware autoregressive reward modeling that handles gradient conflicts across multiple attributes. Evaluation on five safety benchmarks shows improvements in reducing unsafe outputs while maintaining empathetic, human-aligned responses across diverse scenarios, particularly in emotionally charged or high-stakes settings where refusal-only approaches may be counterproductive.
connects: This work extends the line of research addressing limitations in current safety paradigms that rely primarily on refusal mechanisms. It builds on prior work in decoding-time alignment and parameter-efficient safety approaches, moving beyond binary safe/unsafe classifications to nuanced, value-aligned generation. The paper connects to research on reward modeling and attribute-conditioned generation, incorporating techniques from constrained decoding and multi-objective optimization. It responds to recognized tensions between safety and usability—where excessive refusals frustrate users—and engages with work on measuring and optimizing for prosocial behavior. The directional regulation mechanism relates to emerging methods for steering model behavior through latent space interventions, while the preference modeling component builds on recent advances in training reward models that balance multiple conflicting objectives.
enables: This framework opens pathways toward more nuanced, context-aware safety systems that adapt to user needs and emotional dynamics rather than applying uniform restrictions. It enables future work on personalizable safety where systems respect individual value differences while maintaining hard boundaries on genuine harms. The lexicographic constrained generation approach could extend to other multi-objective generation problems beyond safety. The combination of directional vectors and preference modeling provides infrastructure for real-time alignment without retraining, potentially supporting rapid iteration on safety policies as new failure modes emerge. Future directions include scaling the method to larger model families, exploring user-specific alignment preferences, and extending hard constraints to capture more nuanced categories of harm beyond simple classification, positioning test-time alignment as a practical alternative to full model retraining.
